walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Site Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Imhotep set up. Find the five site boards: Market, Pyramids, Temple, Burial Chamber, and Obelisks. Each board is double-sided with an A side and a B side. For your first game, use the A sides. Lay all five boards out in a row where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Scoring Track
walkthrough.text: Place the scoring track near the site boards. Each player will track their points on this throughout the game. Everyone starts at zero. You will score points during the game from the Temple and at the end of the game from all other sites.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Boats
walkthrough.text: Find the eight boat tiles. These have different sizes, holding one to four stones each. Shuffle them face down. Each round, you will draw a specific number of boats based on player count. For now, draw the boats for round one and place them in a row between the players and the site boards.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Round Cards
walkthrough.text: The game lasts six rounds. Shuffle the round cards and set them in a face-down stack. These cards determine which boats are available each round. Also shuffle the market cards and set them aside for when boats arrive at the Market site.

walkthrough.title: Claim Your Stones
walkthrough.text: Each player takes thirty wooden stone blocks in their chosen color. These represent your building materials. Place five stones on your personal supply sled, which is the small area right in front of you. The remaining twenty-five stones go into your quarry, which is your general reserve.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a random first player. Play proceeds clockwise. The game lasts six rounds, and each round continues until all available boats have been sailed to site boards. Ready to build some monuments? Let's learn the rules.

Use A sides for first game (B sides for experienced players)
Lay all 5 boards in a row, accessible to all players

bullets: 8 boat tiles, sizes 1-4 stones

Round cards determine available boats each round

bullets: Random first player, clockwise turn order
6 rounds total
Round ends when all boats have been sailed

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Imhotep is a strategy game about loading stone blocks onto boats and sailing them to building sites. The big twist is that anyone can sail any boat to any site. So your carefully loaded stones might end up somewhere you did not plan. The game lasts six rounds, and the player with the most points at the end wins.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: One Action
walkthrough.text: You can get new stones from your quarry. You can load one stone onto a boat. You can sail a boat to a site. Or you can play a blue market card you previously earned. Let's walk through each action in detail.

walkthrough.title: Action: Get New Stones
walkthrough.text: Take three stones from your quarry and add them to your supply sled. That is it. This is a simple action but essential because you need stones on your sled before you can load them onto boats. If you have fewer than three stones in your quarry, take whatever is left.

walkthrough.title: Action: Load a Stone
walkthrough.text: Take one stone from your supply sled and place it on the next empty space of any boat. Boats have one to four spaces arranged in a line. You always fill from front to back, so your stone goes in the first open slot. The order stones are loaded matters because they unload in the same order at the building site.

walkthrough.title: Action: Sail a Boat
walkthrough.text: Choose any boat that has met its minimum load requirement and sail it to any site board that has not received a boat this round. Each boat has a minimum load, shown by a line on the boat tile. A four-space boat might need at least two stones to sail. Once you sail the boat, stones are unloaded front to back and placed at the site.

walkthrough.title: The Key Twist: Anyone Can Sail
walkthrough.text: Here is what makes Imhotep special. You do not need to have your own stones on a boat to sail it. You can sail a boat loaded entirely with your opponent's stones and send them wherever you want. This means loading your stones is risky because someone else might sail that boat to a site that benefits them instead of you.

walkthrough.title: Building Site: Market
walkthrough.text: When a boat arrives at the Market, stones are unloaded front to back. Each stone's owner takes a market card from the available face-up cards. Market cards come in three types. Blue cards give you special actions you can use on future turns. Green cards score bonus points at the end of the game. Purple cards provide immediate effects.

walkthrough.title: Building Site: Pyramids
walkthrough.text: Stones at the Pyramids are placed in layers to build a pyramid structure. The first layer is a three-by-three grid, then a two-by-two layer on top, then a single stone at the peak. Scoring is based on which stones are visible from above at the end of the game. Stones buried under other stones do not score.

walkthrough.title: Building Site: Temple
walkthrough.text: The Temple is unique because it scores at the end of every round, not just at the end of the game. Stones are placed in rows on the Temple board. At the end of each round, only the top visible stones in each column score points. Getting your stones on top is the key to consistent Temple scoring.

walkthrough.title: Building Site: Burial Chamber
walkthrough.text: Stones at the Burial Chamber are placed in a grid pattern. At the end of the game, you score points based on connected groups of your stones. A group of one scores one point, a group of two scores three points, and larger groups score exponentially more. Building large connected clusters is extremely valuable here.

walkthrough.title: Building Site: Obelisks
walkthrough.text: Each player builds their own obelisk by stacking stones vertically. At the end of the game, the player with the tallest obelisk scores the most points, second tallest scores second most, and so on. Once you start building an obelisk, you want to keep adding to it consistently.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: A round ends when all available boats have been sailed to sites. Score the Temple for this round. Then set up the next round by drawing new boats and market cards. After six rounds, the game ends and you do final scoring for Pyramids, Burial Chamber, Obelisks, and any green market cards. Highest total score wins.

bullets: Load stones onto boats, sail them to building sites
Twist: anyone can sail any boat to any site
6 rounds, highest score wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Ancient Egypt
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns of a three-player game. We will show you how loading, sailing, and the building sites work. We will also demonstrate the key twist of Imhotep, where other players can sail your boats. Follow along and you will be ready to build monuments.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Setup
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with five stones on their supply sled and twenty-five in their quarry. Three boats are available this round, with sizes holding two, three, and four stones. The five building sites are laid out. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Load a Stone
walkthrough.text: They are hoping to fill this boat with their stones and sail it to the Pyramids. Loading is straightforward, but remember, anyone can sail this boat once it meets its minimum load.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Get New Stones
walkthrough.text: Player 2 decides to stock up first. They take three stones from their quarry to their supply sled, bringing their total to eight stones ready to load. Having a full sled gives you flexibility to load quickly on future turns.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Load a Stone
walkthrough.text: Now the boat has two stones from two different players. The minimum load for this boat is two stones, so it can now be sailed by anyone on their turn. The tension builds.

walkthrough.title: The Big Move: Sailing Someone's Boat
walkthrough.text: Player 2 sees the three-space boat with stones from Player 1 and Player 3. Instead of loading their own stone, Player 2 sails that boat to the Market. Player 1 wanted it to go to the Pyramids, but Player 2 sent it to the Market instead. Player 1's stone ends up at the Market, giving them a market card they did not plan for. This is the heart of Imhotep.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Unload Order
walkthrough.text: When the boat docked at the Market, stones were unloaded front to back. Player 1's stone was loaded first, so it unloaded first, giving Player 1 first pick of the market cards. Player 3's stone unloaded second. Load order matters because being first at a site often gives you the best position or best card.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Loading Without a Plan
walkthrough.text: Player 3 loads a stone onto a new boat without thinking about who might sail it and where. On the very next turn, Player 1 sails that boat to the Obelisks, a site where Player 3 has no presence. Player 3's stone is now stuck building someone else's preferred monument.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Think Before You Load
walkthrough.text: Every time you load a stone onto a boat, ask yourself: what happens if someone else sails this boat? Where would they send it? If the worst-case destination is acceptable to you, go ahead and load. If not, consider loading on a different boat or getting new stones instead. In Imhotep, loading is a commitment with uncertain outcomes.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring the Temple
walkthrough.text: Two rounds have passed and Player 2 has not sent any stones to the Temple. Meanwhile, Players 1 and 3 have been scoring two to three points per round from Temple scoring. Over six rounds, that is twelve to eighteen easy points that Player 2 is missing out on.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Temple Scores Every Round
walkthrough.text: The Temple is the only site that scores at the end of every round. Even a single stone in a good position on the Temple can earn you points six times over the course of the game. Do not neglect the Temple. Getting at least a few stones there early provides a steady stream of points that adds up significantly.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Neglecting the Supply Sled
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has been loading aggressively and now has zero stones on their supply sled. They want to load onto a key boat, but they cannot because they have nothing to load. They are forced to spend their turn getting new stones while their opponents take control of the boats.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Stones in Reserve
walkthrough.text: Never let your supply sled run completely empty. Having stones ready to load gives you flexibility to react to what other players do. If you have no stones and a perfect loading opportunity appears, you will lose a full turn restocking. Try to keep at least two or three stones on your sled at all times.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Sailing
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has learned from the earlier rounds. They load their stone as the last one on a boat, then immediately sail it on their next turn to the Burial Chamber, where they are building a connected cluster. By being the last to load and the first to sail, Player 2 maintained complete control over where that boat went.

walkthrough.title: Continue the Game
walkthrough.text: Play continues for all six rounds. Each round, new boats appear and new market cards are revealed. The key decisions remain the same: when to load, when to sail, and where to send the boats. Every action affects multiple players because anyone can sail any boat. Keep balancing your plans with the threat of opponent interference.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have walked through your first practice session of Imhotep. You have seen how loading, sailing, and unloading work, and you have learned from three common beginner mistakes: loading without thinking about where the boat might go, ignoring the Temple's round-by-round scoring, and running out of stones on your sled. You are ready to build monuments.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your next game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on boat manipulation and site prioritization.

Second, reference the Appendix for scoring details on each building site.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the most common mistakes in Imhotep. First, loading stones without considering where opponents might sail the boat. Second, ignoring the Temple and its valuable round-by-round scoring. Third, running your supply sled dry and wasting a turn restocking. Fourth, focusing on only one or two sites while ignoring the others. Keep these in mind to build a stronger game.

walkthrough.title: Control Through Sailing
walkthrough.text: The most powerful action in Imhotep is sailing, not loading. When you sail a boat, you decide where everyone's stones on that boat end up. The ideal play is to load your stone last and sail the boat immediately on your next turn. This gives you maximum control because you choose the destination before anyone can add more stones or redirect the boat themselves.

walkthrough.title: Denial Sailing
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best move is sailing a boat loaded with your opponent's stones to a site that does not help them. If an opponent has loaded three stones on a boat headed for the Pyramids, sailing that boat to the Market instead denies them their planned points. This is called denial sailing, and it is one of the strongest competitive moves in the game.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Neglect Any Site
walkthrough.text: Every building site offers significant points. Focusing exclusively on one site while ignoring the others means your opponents score freely everywhere else. Aim to have at least some presence at every site. The Temple is especially important because it scores every round. The Burial Chamber rewards connected groups, which can explode in points if you plan ahead.

walkthrough.title: Load Order Matters
walkthrough.text: The first stone loaded on a boat is the first stone unloaded at the site. At the Market, first unloaded means first pick of the available cards. At the Pyramids, first unloaded means you get placed in the best scoring positions. At the Burial Chamber, first unloaded lets you choose where your stone goes in the grid. Loading early gives you positional advantage, but it also gives opponents more time to redirect the boat.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Supply Sled
walkthrough.text: Keep two to three stones on your supply sled at all times. Running out of stones forces you to spend a turn restocking while your opponents load and sail. Conversely, spending every turn getting stones and never loading wastes time. The ideal rhythm is to load a few stones, sail when the opportunity is right, and restock before you run empty.

walkthrough.title: Market Card Value
walkthrough.text: Market cards are more powerful than they look. Blue cards give you extra actions on future turns, effectively giving you a bonus turn. Green cards score points at end of game based on conditions you can plan around. Do not dismiss the Market site. A well-timed Market visit can net you a card worth five or more points, which is as good as any building site placement.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Clock
walkthrough.text: The game lasts exactly six rounds, so you need to pace your efforts. In the early rounds, focus on establishing presence at the Temple for recurring points and building the foundation of your Burial Chamber clusters. In the middle rounds, push for Pyramids and Obelisks. In the final rounds, prioritize whichever sites will give you the most end-game points. Every stone counts, so do not waste turns.

bullets: Don't load without considering where opponents might sail
Don't ignore the Temple — it scores every round
Don't run your supply sled empty — wastes a full turn
Don't focus on just 1-2 sites — diversify

walkthrough.title: Boat Manipulation Mastery
walkthrough.text: The highest level of Imhotep play revolves around boat manipulation. The ideal sequence is: load your stone as the last stone on a boat, then sail it on your next turn before anyone else can react. This gives you control over where the boat goes. If you cannot sail immediately, at least make sure the worst-case destination for your stone is still acceptable.

walkthrough.title: Burial Chamber Networks
walkthrough.text: The Burial Chamber rewards connected groups exponentially. A group of three scores significantly more than three individual stones. The advanced play is to plan your Burial Chamber placements across multiple rounds, ensuring each new stone connects to your existing cluster. Since unload order determines placement, you need to be first on boats headed to the Burial Chamber to choose optimal grid positions.

walkthrough.title: Temple Dominance
walkthrough.text: The Temple scores every round, making it the most consistent point source. Advanced players fight for top positions in Temple columns by carefully timing when they send boats there. Sending a boat to the Temple later in a round can place your stones on top of opponents' stones, claiming the scoring position. The Temple alone can account for twenty or more points over six rounds if you control the top spots.

walkthrough.title: Obelisk Commitment
walkthrough.text: Once you start building an obelisk, you are committed. The tallest obelisk scores the most, but falling behind in height means your investment is less efficient. The advanced play is to decide early whether you are going all-in on obelisks or ignoring them. Half-hearted obelisk building wastes stones. If you commit, send every possible stone there to guarantee the tallest tower.

walkthrough.title: Pyramid Positioning
walkthrough.text: At the Pyramids, only visible stones score. Stones in the bottom layer can be covered by stones in the layer above. Advanced players place stones in the upper layers where they cannot be buried. Being unloaded later at the Pyramids can actually be advantageous because later stones go on top of earlier ones. Sometimes loading last on a Pyramids boat gives you the best scoring position.

walkthrough.title: B-Side Site Strategies
walkthrough.text: Once you are comfortable with the A sides, the B-side site boards change the scoring rules and create new strategic dimensions. B-side Pyramids has a different layering structure. B-side Burial Chamber changes the grid pattern. The B sides generally reward more aggressive and focused play. Explore them once you have mastered the fundamentals.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to where your opponents want their stones to go. If someone has been building a large Burial Chamber cluster, they will want boats to go there. Use this knowledge to deny them by sailing boats elsewhere or by loading your stones on their preferred boats to dilute their strategy. The metagame of reading intentions and countering them is where Imhotep truly shines.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final two rounds, every action must directly contribute to points. Calculate where your stones will score the most and focus exclusively on those sites. If your Burial Chamber cluster needs one more stone to jump from three to six points, prioritize that. If your obelisk is one stone behind the leader, send everything there. The endgame is about precision, not exploration.

entries.definition: A building site where stones are unloaded to claim market cards. Cards come in three types: blue for future actions, green for end-game points, and purple for immediate effects. First unloaded gets first pick.

A building site where stones are placed in layers to form a pyramid structure. Bottom layer is three by three, middle is two by two, peak is one stone. Only visible stones from above score at game end.
A building site that scores at the end of every round. Stones are placed in rows, and only the top visible stone in each column scores. The only site with recurring scoring. Consistent point source.

A building site where stones are placed in a grid. At game end, score based on connected groups of your color. Larger groups score exponentially more points. Planning connectivity across rounds is essential.
A building site where each player stacks stones vertically to build an obelisk. At game end, tallest obelisk scores the most points, second tallest scores second most, and so on. Requires commitment.
A tile with one to four spaces for loading stones. Boats have a minimum load requirement before they can be sailed. Any player can sail any boat once the minimum is met. Stones unload front to back.
Wooden blocks in player colors. Thirty per player. Used to build at all five sites. Stones start in the quarry and move to the supply sled before being loaded onto boats.
Your personal supply area holding stones ready to load. Starts with five stones. Refilled by taking the Get New Stones action. Never let it run empty.
Your general reserve of unused stones. Starts with twenty-five stones. When you take the Get New Stones action, you move three stones from quarry to supply sled.
A card gained from the Market site that provides a special action usable on a future turn. Playing a blue market card is one of the four turn actions. Effectively gives you a bonus action.
A card gained from the Market site that scores bonus points at the end of the game based on specific conditions. Some reward having stones at certain sites or having collected certain card types.
A card gained from the Market site that provides an immediate effect when received. Effects vary but can include extra stone placements or bonus resources.
Determines which boats and how many are available each round. The game uses six round cards for six rounds. Different combinations of boats create different strategic situations each round.
One of four turn actions. Take three stones from your quarry and add them to your supply sled. If fewer than three remain in your quarry, take whatever is left.
One of four turn actions. Place one stone from your supply sled onto the next empty space of any boat. Stones fill from front to back. Load order determines unload order at the site.
One of four turn actions. Choose a boat that meets its minimum load and send it to any site that has not received a boat this round. Any player can sail any boat. Stones unload front to back.
One of four turn actions. Use a previously collected blue market card for its special effect. The card is then discarded.
Each boat has a minimum number of stones required before it can be sailed. Shown by a line on the boat tile. A four-space boat might require two or three stones minimum.

Stones unload from a boat in the order they were loaded, front to back. First loaded is first placed at the building site. This order determines card picks at Market and placement positions at other sites.
An advanced strategy where you sail a boat loaded with opponents' stones to a site that does not benefit them. Redirecting opponents' planned placements is one of the strongest competitive moves.
At the Burial Chamber, a set of your stones that are orthogonally adjacent to each other in the grid. Larger connected groups score exponentially more points at end of game.
Each building site board is double-sided. A sides use standard scoring rules for learning. B sides introduce alternate scoring rules for experienced players, creating new strategic dimensions.
A shared track where players mark their current score. Temple points are tracked during the game. All other site scores are tallied at game end.
